name: Resonance
description: Breathe at the body's natural resonant frequency (~5.5 breaths per minute) to maximize heart rate variability. This is the 'perfect breath' rate — the same rhythm produced by Ave Maria prayer and Sanskrit mantras — synchronizing cardiovascular and respiratory systems into coherence.

name: Deep Calm
description: At just 3.75 breaths per minute, this ultra-slow rhythm activates deep parasympathetic dominance — shifting the nervous system into its lowest gear for profound relaxation and recovery.

Dr. Andrew Weil's natural tranquilizer for the nervous system. The 7-second hold saturates blood with oxygen while the 8-second exhale maximizes CO₂ expulsion and vagal activation. Widely adopted by military for rapid sleep onset — the 19-second cycle at ~3 bpm creates extreme parasympathetic dominance.

name: Box
description: Equal inhale, hold, exhale, and hold phases create a perfectly balanced rhythm used by Navy SEALs to maintain calm and focus under pressure. Scale the duration to your CO₂ tolerance: 3s per phase if low, 5–6s if moderate, 8–10s if high.

name: Extended Exhale
description: A 1:2 inhale-to-exhale ratio that emphasizes the exhale to activate the body's rest-and-digest response. The extended exhalation phase stimulates the vagus nerve more powerfully than equal-ratio breathing, making this one of the simplest and most effective calming patterns.

With an exhale nearly 60% longer than the inhale at just 3.3 breaths per minute, this creates one of the strongest vagal activation ratios of any simple breathing technique. Described by the Human Givens Institute as the most powerful single relaxation technique available.

name: Physiological Sigh
description: A double inhale followed by a long exhale — the fastest real-time method to reduce stress, validated by Stanford research. The double inhale reopens collapsed alveoli in the lungs, maximizing CO₂ offloading in a single breath cycle. Use 1–3 breaths as an acute rescue tool.

name: Cyclic Sighing
description: Stanford's landmark 2023 protocol: 5 minutes of continuous double-inhale-then-long-exhale breathing that outperformed mindfulness meditation for mood improvement in a randomized controlled trial. Same mechanism as the Physiological Sigh, but sustained as a daily practice with cumulative benefits over 28+ days.

name: Longer Holds
description: Progressively lengthens each breath cycle using an automatic ramp, training the body to slow down and deepen its natural breathing rhythm — each round stretches slightly longer than the last.

name: Study
description: Starts with calm breathing that gradually quickens to sharpen focus, then returns to a slower pace. Designed for sustained concentration during long work or study sessions.

name: Breathe Light
description: Patrick McKeown's core Oxygen Advantage exercise. Gently reduce breathing volume to create tolerable air hunger, allowing CO₂ to accumulate and improve the Bohr effect — the mechanism by which higher CO₂ causes hemoglobin to release more oxygen to tissues. Breathing should be so quiet that fine hairs inside your nostrils barely move.

name: Breath of Fire
description: Rapid, rhythmic breathing from Kundalini yoga. Builds energy, increases alertness, and strengthens the diaphragm through sustained diaphragmatic pumping followed by a long retention that concentrates the energizing effect.

name: Bellow Breathing
description: Rapid double-inhale and exhale cycles that ramp in intensity, followed by a deep inhale and an extended hold that grows longer each round. A progressive engine that builds heat and energy.

name: Brown Fat Activation
description: Deliberate breathing with emphasis on strong, forceful inhales and breath holds to stimulate brown adipose tissue and generate body heat. The inhale-dominant ratio (3.5s in vs 1.5s out) creates a sympathetic charge that primes thermogenesis.

name: Tummo
description: A Tibetan inner heat meditation practice. Vigorous inhale-dominant breathing followed by extended breath holds builds core body temperature and focuses the mind. Traditionally practiced in freezing conditions to demonstrate mastery over autonomic function.

name: Wim Hof
description: The Wim Hof Method: controlled hyperventilation followed by a breath hold on empty lungs, then a recovery breath with a short hold. A 2025 trial (N=404) confirmed WHM produces greater momentary improvements in energy, mental clarity, and stress handling compared to meditation.

name: CO2 Tolerance
description: Gradually lengthens exhales across three stages to train your body's tolerance to carbon dioxide, improving breath hold capacity and overall breathing efficiency over time.

name: Apnea
description: Breath hold training with progressively longer retention times. Builds comfort with air hunger and trains the dive reflex for freediving and general breath control.

name: Retention
description: Simple breath retention practice with holds after each exhale. An accessible entry point to breath-hold training that builds CO₂ tolerance and calms the mind.

name: CO₂ Table
description: The standard freediving hypercapnic protocol: hold duration stays constant while rest periods shrink each round, progressively training the brain to tolerate rising carbon dioxide — the primary limiter in breath-hold performance. 8 rounds, increasingly uncomfortable.

name: O₂ Table
description: The complementary freediving hypoxic protocol: rest stays constant while breath-hold duration increases each round, progressively depleting oxygen and training the body's hypoxic response — building adaptations that extend breath-hold capacity.

name: Shamanic
description: Progressive box breathing with increasing durations and extended holds between rounds. A meditative journey that deepens with each cycle, building from 4-count to 8-count box patterns.

name: Altered State
description: Intense, rhythmic hyperventilation followed by a long breath hold to shift consciousness and induce a meditative altered state. The rapid breathing depletes CO₂, and the extended hold creates a unique neurological window as oxygen levels drop.

name: Dopamine Light
description: An accelerating breath pattern that builds intensity through progressively faster cycles, culminating in a long hold designed to elevate mood and energy through catecholamine release.

name: Holotropic
description: Based on Stanislav Grof's holotropic breathwork. Sustained deep, fast breathing with a ramp that accelerates the pace, pushing toward expanded states of awareness through prolonged hyperventilation and an open-ended hold.

name: Nadi Shodhana
description: Alternate nostril breathing in the classical 1:2:2 pranayama ratio. Alternating airflow between nostrils balances sympathetic and parasympathetic tone, harmonizing left and right brain hemispheres while significantly reducing blood pressure.

seq.text: Close right nostril, inhale through left
Close left nostril, exhale through right
Keep left closed, inhale through right
Close right nostril, exhale through left
Release both nostrils. Breathe normally through both sides

name: Bhramari
description: The Humming Bee Breath. A continuous humming vibration during exhalation stimulates the vagus nerve and boosts nasal nitric oxide production by 15× compared to quiet exhaling — one of the most potent single-technique vagal toners in all of pranayama.

seq.text: Close ears with thumbs. Hum on exhale — mouth closed, teeth slightly apart
Release your ears. Sit still and listen to the fading resonance

name: Sitali
description: Drawing air across a curled tongue creates evaporative cooling that lowers core body temperature, reduces blood pressure, and shifts brainwaves toward calming alpha and theta patterns — one of the only pranayamas that intentionally uses mouth inhalation.

seq.text: Curl tongue edges upward to form a tube. Inhale through tongue like sipping a straw
Close mouth. Return to normal nasal breathing

name: Viloma
description: Interrupted breathing developed by B.K.S. Iyengar. The inhale is split into three segments with 2-second pauses between each, expanding lung capacity section by section while teaching precise breath control — like filling a glass from bottom to top.

name: Sniper Breathing
description: A two-phase military protocol: first calm the nervous system with box breathing, then exploit the natural respiratory pause at the bottom of exhalation — the 3–7 second window where the body is stillest and heart rate variability is minimized. Used by snipers and SWAT operators for maximum precision.

seq.text: Phase 2: Precision mode — hold at the bottom of your exhale

name: SOMA Awakening
description: A multi-phase protocol combining rhythmic breathing, exhale retention, and inhale retention with body locks. A Cambridge University study found this pattern increases Lempel-Ziv neural complexity — the same brain-state metric elevated by psychedelic substances — producing reported experiences of bliss and expanded awareness.

seq.text: Exhale completely and hold on empty lungs
Inhale deeply and hold — squeeze pelvic floor, chin to chest
Let your breathing return to its own natural rhythm

name: Hook Recovery
description: The critical post-breath-hold recovery technique used by freedivers and fighter pilots. A sharp inhale followed by a brief pressure hold forces oxygen into the bloodstream and pushes blood toward the brain, recovering arterial oxygen 2× faster than passive breathing. Use after any apnea exercise.

seq.text: Recovery breaths: sharp inhale, bear down gently, release
